source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
The "charmed" particles observed in $e^{+} e^{-}$storage rings have not yet been seen in hadron-hadron interactions. One possible means for detecting such particles is the observation of muons resulting from their leptonic decays. For example, consider a charmed particle $c$ with decay mode 
$$ c \rightarrow \mu 
u $$ Unfortunately, the experimental situation is complicated by the presence of muons from $\pi$ decays. Consider an experiment at Fermilab in which 400 GeV protons strike a thick iron target (beam dump) as depicted in Fig. 4.13. Some of the muons entering the detector will be from $\pi$ decays and some from $c$ decays (ignore other processes). Calculate the ratio of muons from $c$ decays to those from $\pi$ decays under the following assumptions: the pions that have suffered interaction in the dump completely disappear from the beam, the energy spectra of both $\pi$ and $c$ are flat from minimum up to the maximum possible energy, the mass of the $c$ is $2 \mathrm{GeV} / c^{2}$ and its lifetime is $\ll 10^{-10} \mathrm{sec}$, one can ignore muon energy loss in the iron, one can ignore any complications due to the geometry of the muon detector, the $p-p$ inelastic cross section is 30 mb , and the mean charged pion multiplicity in inelastic interactions is 8 . Be specific. State any additional assumptions. Give a numerical value for the ratio at $E_{\mu}=100 \mathrm{GeV}$ assuming the total production cross section for $c$ to be $10 \mu b$ per Fe nucleus and that it decays to $\mu 
u 10 \%$ of the time.

image_dependency: 题干显式提到 Fig. 4.13，但同时 `requires_image=false` 且 `text_dominant=true`。
题目已在文字中给出主要物理设定与近似条件，且明确可忽略探测器几何复杂性，降低了对图示的依赖。
图像已登记存在且清晰度尚可，但从字段证据看更像辅助背景而非解题必要输入。
multi_step: 需要综合多个条件：束流-靶相互作用、π 在 dump 中相互作用/衰变、c 产生截面、分支比、能谱假设，以及在给定 `E_mu=100 GeV` 下比较两类来源。
题目要求计算的是来源比值而非单一步代入，涉及至少两步以上的物理建模与数量级计算。
题干较长且包含多项显式假设与额外假设要求，表明解题过程具有明显多阶段推理特征。
verifiability: 答案是明确的数值型短答案 `$4 \times 10^{-3}$`，便于后续核验。
题目给出了关键数值参数（截面、分支比、能量点、质量、寿命、乘数等），可据此复算目标比值。
虽然题目允许补充假设，存在一定建模自由度，但最终输出是单一数值，整体仍较易验证。
`registry_passed=true`，题目与答案资产完整，支持后续规则化检查。

normalization_record.normalized_question_text: The "charmed" particles observed in $e^{+} e^{-}$ storage rings have not yet been seen in hadron-hadron interactions. One possible means for detecting such particles is the observation of muons resulting from their leptonic decays. For example, consider a charmed particle $c$ with decay mode $$c \rightarrow \mu \nu$$. Unfortunately, the experimental situation is complicated by the presence of muons from $\pi$ decays. Consider an experiment at Fermilab in which 400 GeV protons strike a thick iron target (beam dump) as depicted in Fig. 4.13. Some of the muons entering the detector will be from $\pi$ decays and some from $c$ decays (ignore other processes). Calculate the ratio of muons from $c$ decays to those from $\pi$ decays under the following assumptions: the pions that have suffered interaction in the dump completely disappear from the beam; the energy spectra of both $\pi$ and $c$ are flat from minimum up to the maximum possible energy; the mass of the $c$ is $2 \, \mathrm{GeV}/c^{2}$ and its lifetime is $\ll 10^{-10} \, \mathrm{sec}$; one can ignore muon energy loss in the iron; one can ignore any complications due to the geometry of the muon detector; the $p$-$p$ inelastic cross section is 30 mb; and the mean charged pion multiplicity in inelastic interactions is 8. Be specific. State any additional assumptions. Give a numerical value for the ratio at $E_{\mu} = 100 \, \mathrm{GeV}$ assuming the total production cross section for $c$ to be $10 \, \mu\mathrm{b}$ per Fe nucleus and that it decays to $\mu \nu$ 10% of the time.
normalization_notes: Removed minor spacing and punctuation noise while preserving the original physics content.
Retained the figure reference in the text, but the problem is text-solvable because detector geometry is explicitly said to be ignorable.
No choice options were provided, so normalized_choice_map is empty.

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text:
The "charmed" particles observed in $e^{+} e^{-}$storage rings have not yet been seen in hadron-hadron interactions. One possible means for detecting such particles is the observation of muons resulting from their leptonic decays. For example, consider a charmed particle $c$ with decay mode 
$$ c \rightarrow \mu 
u $$ Unfortunately, the experimental situation is complicated by the presence of muons from $\pi$ decays. Consider an experiment at Fermilab in which 400 GeV protons strike a thick iron target (beam dump) as depicted in Fig. 4.13. Some of the muons entering the detector will be from $\pi$ decays and some from $c$ decays (ignore other processes). Calculate the ratio of muons from $c$ decays to those from $\pi$ decays under the following assumptions: the pions that have suffered interaction in the dump completely disappear from the beam, the energy spectra of both $\pi$ and $c$ are flat from minimum up to the maximum possible energy, the mass of the $c$ is $2 \mathrm{GeV} / c^{2}$ and its lifetime is $\ll 10^{-10} \mathrm{sec}$, one can ignore muon energy loss in the iron, one can ignore any complications due to the geometry of the muon detector, the $p-p$ inelastic cross section is 30 mb , and the mean charged pion multiplicity in inelastic interactions is 8 . Be specific. State any additional assumptions. Give a numerical value for the ratio at $E_{\mu}=100 \mathrm{GeV}$ assuming the total production cross section for $c$ to be $10 \mu b$ per Fe nucleus and that it decays to $\mu 
u 10 \%$ of the time.
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

metadata.reasoning_chain:
In addition to the assumptions listed in the question, we also assume the charge independence of nucleon interactions so that $\sigma_{p p}=\sigma_{p n}$ and the mean charged-pion multiplicities are the same for $p p$ and $p n$ collisions.

For ${ }^{56} \mathrm{Fe}$, the number densities of protons and neutrons are the same, being
$$
N_{p}=N_{n}=\frac{28}{56} \times 7.8 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}=2.35 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}
$$

Denote the flux of protons in the incident beam by $\phi(x)$, where $x$ is the target thickness from the surface of incidence. As
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d \phi}{d x} & =-\left(\sigma_{p p} N_{p}+\sigma_{p n} N_{n}\right) \phi=-2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} \phi 
\phi & =\phi_{0} e^{-2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} x}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the target is sufficiently thick, say $x=10 \mathrm{~m}=10^{3} \mathrm{~cm}$, then
$$
\phi=\phi_{0} \exp \left(-2 \times 30 \times 10^{-27} \times 2.35 \times 10^{24} \times 10^{3}\right)=5.8 \times 10^{-62} \phi_{0},
$$
at the exit surface, showing that the beam of protons is completely dissipated in the target. This will be assumed in the following.

Consider first the $c$ quarks produced in $p-\mathrm{Fe}$ interactions in the target. From the given data $\sigma_{p \mathrm{Fe}}(c)=10 \mu b, \sigma_{p p}=30 \mathrm{mb}$, we find the number of $c$ quarks so produced as
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{c} & =\int N_{\mathrm{Fe}} \sigma(c) d \phi \approx N_{\mathrm{Fe}} \sigma(c) \phi_{0} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} x} d x 
& =\frac{N_{\mathrm{Fe}}}{2 N_{p p}} \frac{\sigma(c)}{\sigma_{p p}} \phi_{0}=\frac{1}{56} \times \frac{10^{-5}}{30 \times 10^{-3}} \phi_{0}=5.95 \times 10^{-6} \phi_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

As the $c$ quarks have lifetime $\ll 10^{-10}$ s, all those produced in $p-\mathrm{Fe}$ interactions will decay in the target, giving rise to muons $10 \%$ of the times. Thus
$$
N_{\mu c}=0.1 N_{c}=5.95 \times 10^{-7} \phi_{0} .
$$

Next consider the muons arising from the decay of charged pions produced in $p$-nucleon interactions. After emission the pions may interact
with the nucleons of the target and disappear from the beam, as assumed, or decay in flight giving rise to muons. For the former case we assume $\sigma_{\pi p}=\sigma_{\pi n} \approx \frac{2}{3} \sigma_{p p}=20 \mathrm{mb}$ at high energies. For the latter case the lifetime of the charged pions in the laboratory is $\gamma_{\pi} / \lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the decay constant and $\gamma_{\pi}=\left(1-\beta_{\pi}^{2}\right)^{-\frac{1}{2}}, \beta_{\pi} c$ being the mean velocity of the pions. Then the change of $N_{\pi}$ per unit interval of $x$ is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d N_{\pi}}{d x} & =8\left(\sigma_{p p} N_{p}+\sigma_{p n} N_{n}\right) \phi(x)-\left(\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c}+\sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}+\sigma_{\pi n} N_{n}\right) N_{\pi} 
& =16 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} \varphi_{0} e^{-2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p} x}-\left(\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c}+2 \sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}\right) N_{\pi} 
& =8 B \phi_{0} e^{-B x}-B^{\prime} N_{\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $B=2 \sigma_{p p} N_{p}, B^{\prime}=2 \sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}+\lambda^{\prime}, \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi c}}$. The solution of the differential equation is
$$
N_{\pi}=\frac{8 B}{B^{\prime}-B}\left(e^{-B x}-e^{-B^{\prime} x}\right) \phi_{0} .
$$

Hence the number of charged pions which decay in the target per unit interval of $x$ is
$$
\frac{d N_{\pi}(\lambda)}{d x}=\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c} N_{\pi}(\lambda)=\frac{8 B \lambda^{\prime}}{B^{\prime}-B}\left(e^{-B x}-e^{-B^{\prime} x}\right) \phi_{0} .
$$

Integration from $x=0$ to $x=\infty$ gives
$$
N_{\pi}(\lambda)=\frac{8 B \lambda^{\prime}}{B^{\prime}-B}\left(\frac{1}{B}-\frac{1}{B^{\prime}}\right) \phi_{0}=\frac{8 \lambda^{\prime} \phi_{0}}{B^{\prime}} .
$$

The branching ratio for $\pi \rightarrow \mu 
u \approx 100 \%$, so that $N_{\mu \pi} \approx N_{\pi}(\lambda)$. This means that the energy spectrum of muons is also flat (though in actual fact high-energy muons are more likely than low-energy ones), making the comparison with $N_{\mu c}$ much simpler.

Take for example $E_{\mu} \sim 100 \mathrm{GeV}$. Then $E_{\pi} \gtrsim 100 \mathrm{GeV}, \beta_{\pi} \approx 1, \gamma_{\pi} \gtrsim$ 714 , and so
$$
\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c}=\frac{1}{2.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 714 \times 3 \times 10^{10}}=1.8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}
$$
As
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\pi p} N_{p} & =20 \times 10^{-27} \times 2.34 \times 10^{24}=4.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \gg \lambda^{\prime} 
N_{\pi}(\lambda) & \simeq \frac{8 \lambda^{\prime} \phi_{0}}{2 \sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}}=\frac{8 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-6} \phi_{0}}{2 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-2}}=1.5 \times 10^{-4} \phi_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence
$$
\frac{N_{\mu c}}{N_{\mu \pi}}=\frac{5.95 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}=4 \times 10^{-3}
$$

u 10 \%$ of the time.

\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\gamma_{\pi} \beta_{\pi} c}=\frac{1}{2.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 714 \times 3 \times 10^{10}}=1.8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}

\sigma_{\pi p} N_{p} & =20 \times 10^{-27} \times 2.34 \times 10^{24}=4.7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \gg \lambda^{\prime} 
N_{\pi}(\lambda) & \simeq \frac{8 \lambda^{\prime} \phi_{0}}{2 \sigma_{\pi p} N_{p}}=\frac{8 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-6} \phi_{0}}{2 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-2}}=1.5 \times 10^{-4} \phi_{0}
\end{aligned}

\frac{N_{\mu c}}{N_{\mu \pi}}=\frac{5.95 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}=4 \times 10^{-3}

Although the image is not needed, that is not a rejection reason under this gate because the item still has clear semantic annotation value.

The "charmed" particles observed in $e^{+} e^{-}$storage rings have not yet been seen in hadron-hadron interactions. One possible means for detecting such particles is the observation of muons resulting from their leptonic decays. For example, consider a charmed particle $c$ with decay mode 
$$ c \rightarrow \mu 
u $$ Unfortunately, the experimental situation is complicated by the presence of muons from $\pi$ decays. Consider an experiment at Fermilab in which 400 GeV protons strike a thick iron target (beam dump) as depicted in Fig. 4.13. Some of the muons entering the detector will be from $\pi$ decays and some from $c$ decays (ignore other processes). Calculate the ratio of muons from $c$ decays to those from $\pi$ decays under the following assumptions: the pions that have suffered interaction in the dump completely disappear from the beam, the energy spectra of both $\pi$ and $c$ are flat from minimum up to the maximum possible energy, the mass of the $c$ is $2 \mathrm{GeV} / c^{2}$ and its lifetime is $\ll 10^{-10} \mathrm{sec}$, one can ignore muon energy loss in the iron, one can ignore any complications due to the geometry of the muon detector, the $p-p$ inelastic cross section is 30 mb , and the mean charged pion multiplicity in inelastic interactions is 8 . Be specific. State any additional assumptions. Give a numerical value for the ratio at $E_{\mu}=100 \mathrm{GeV}$ assuming the total production cross section for $c$ to be $10 \mu b$ per Fe nucleus and that it decays to $\mu 
u 10 \%$ of the time.

The "charmed" particles observed in $e^{+} e^{-}$storage rings have not yet been seen in hadron-hadron interactions. One possible means for detecting such particles is the observation of muons resulting from their leptonic decays. For example, consider a charmed particle $c$ with decay mode 
$$ c \rightarrow \mu 
u $$ Unfortunately, the experimental situation is complicated by the presence of muons from $\pi$ decays. Consider an experiment at Fermilab in which 400 GeV protons strike a thick iron target (beam dump) as depicted in Fig. 4.13. Some of the muons entering the detector will be from $\pi$ decays and some from $c$ decays (ignore other processes). Calculate the ratio of muons from $c$ decays to those from $\pi$ decays under the following assumptions: the pions that have suffered interaction in the dump completely disappear from the beam, the energy spectra of both $\pi$ and $c$ are flat from minimum up to the maximum possible energy, the mass of the $c$ is $2 \mathrm{GeV} / c^{2}$ and its lifetime is $\ll 10^{-10} \mathrm{sec}$, one can ignore muon energy loss in the iron, one can ignore any complications due to the geometry of the muon detector, the $p-p$ inelastic cross section is 30 mb , and the mean charged pion multiplicity in inelastic interactions is 8 . Be specific. State any additional assumptions. Give a numerical value for the ratio at $E_{\mu}=100 \mathrm{GeV}$ assuming the total production cross section for $c$ to be $10 \mu b$ per Fe nucleus and that it decays to $\mu 
u 10 \%$ of the time.

agent_assessment.rationale: Fallback understanding judgment is based on semantic field presence and image availability rather than hard metric thresholds.

cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is complete and understandable, the rewritten question is consistent and usable, the expected answer is present, and alignment and solvability reports show no serious failure. Although the image is not needed, that is not a rejection reason under this gate because the item still has clear semantic annotation value.
